data.title: JGBs Consolidate Ahead of Japan's Upper House Elections — Market Talk
data.description: JGBs consolidate ahead of Japan's Upper House elections on Sunday. Surveys surrounding the elections show prospects of the Liberal Democratic Party-Komeito coalition retaining its majority have grown even more uncertain than earlier in the campaign, two members of Barclays' FICC Research say in a research report. "If the fiscally dovish opposition parties fare even better than projected in the pre-election polls, the initial post-election response would likely be an accelerated bear-steepening in JGBs," the members say. The 10-year JGB yield is steady at 1.555%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2010 ET - JGBs consolidate ahead of Japan's Upper House elections on Sunday. Surveys surrounding the elections show prospects of the Liberal Democratic Party-Komeito coalition retaining its majority have grown even more uncertain than earlier in the campaign, two members of Barclays' FICC Research say in a research report. "If the fiscally dovish opposition parties fare even better than projected in the pre-election polls, the initial post-election response would likely be an accelerated bear-steepening in JGBs," the members say. The 10-year JGB yield is steady at 1.555%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1942 ET - Japanese stocks may rise following signs of U.S. economic strength. Nikkei futures are up 0.4% at 40050 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 148.31, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House election on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6% to 39901.19 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1921 ET - Asked in an audience Q&A in Manhattan whether Fed governors are committed to maintaining their independence from political leaders, Fed Gov. Christopher Waller gives a strong affirmative. "I've never met a central banker in my life who didn't cherish it," Waller says of the principle that monetary authorities should get a free hand. Harsh criticism of the Fed from the White House has made central-bank independence a hot-button topic. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1904 ET - Fed governor Christopher Waller swiftly denies that anyone from the Trump administration has talked to him about succeeding Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Waller is speaking before a group of finance pros in Manhattan, laying out the case that the Fed should cut rates in July. In an audience Q&A, an attendee asks him if the Trump administration has approached him about Powell's job. "Nope!" he responds, and moves on to the next question. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1758 ET - Netflix isn't seeing any pain points in the economy from its end. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call said while they're watching consumer sentiment in the broader economy, at this time there's nothing significant to note in the metrics and indicators it gets through its business. This comes as Netflix has cracked down on password sharing, built out an ad-supported offering and raised prices on some subscription tiers. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1745 ET - The venture capital industry's main trade group saluted the House passage of three cryptocurrency-related bills. "These bills will enhance the venture industry's ability to back transformative companies and drive lasting economic growth," said Bobby Franklin, president and CEO of National Venture Capital Association. He said the bills, GENIUS Act, the CLARITY Act, and the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, establish a clear regulatory framework for stablecoins and other digital assets. Crypto startups globally raised $4.9 billion via 446 venture-capital deals in the first quarter of this year, according to research from Galaxy. Of those deals about 39% were for companies based in the U.S. ( yuliya.chernova@wsj.com )

1713 ET - U.S. home sales rose in June from May, marking five consecutive months of gains, RE/MAX Holdings reports. Chicago had the highest number of sales in June, jumping 12.9% from the same time last year, the report says. Buyers and sellers in the windy city's market have finally come to terms with the current housing landscape, RE/MAX premier broker Mike Opyd says. The mental shift has pushed more sellers to offer properties on the market for buyers to jump on before prices rise even further, Opyd says. (natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

1643 ET - Canadian consumer spending remained solid in 2Q, says Royal Bank of Canada economist Rachel Battaglia, based on anonymized cardholder data from the lender. This adds to a series of secondary and tertiary data that suggest the economy has not fallen off a cliff, as commentators and analysts feared due to President Trump's tariff threats. For the April-to-June period, core retail sales — which strips on automobiles and gasoline — rose 3%, based on activity on RBC credit and debit cards. Core retail sales did contract in June from May, according to RBC data, although Battaglia notes May was a particularly strong month. Quarterly spending growth remains positive in nearly all categories, Battaglia says, led by dining and entertainment. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1634 ET - Netflix can thank a weaker dollar for its raised outlook. The streaming service raised its revenue forecast to $44.8 billion to $45.2 billion for the year, adding that the majority of the increase is due to the recent depreciation of the dollar against most other currencies. Momentum in member growth and ad sales rounds out its higher guidance. Shares tick down 0.5% in after hours trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1612 ET - Bank of Nova Scotia's economics team reaffirms its call the Bank of Canada is done cutting rates for this year. Economic activity in Canada looks to be stronger — although still tepid — than what Scotiabank previously believed. It now believes Canada GDP in 2Q to be positive, versus an earlier call of a 1% contraction, citing strong labor data for June and three-straight months of higher existing-home sales. The Scotiabank economics team adds that informal conversations with bank clients suggest a more optimistic view of the economy relative to earlier this year. Scotiabank says the combination of "stronger, but not strong," growth and elevated core inflation will keep the BOC's policy rate steady at 2.75%. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1534 ET - Treasury yields rise as U.S. data indicate layoffs aren't picking up while retail sales remain robust. The indicators weaken the case for fast interest rate cuts by the Fed, as the Trump administration has advocated. The preliminary University of Michigan consumer survey for July, due tomorrow, is expected to tick higher in a WSJ poll, in another potential argument for a longer Fed hold. June housing starts are also forecast to accelerate. The 10-year rises 0.008 percentage point to 4.462% and the two-year increases 0.032 p.p. to 3.916%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1526 ET - Lasting uncertainty is likely to hit U.S. corporate earnings, says Dan Genter, of Genter Capital. He expects an average earnings growth of 9.6% for the S&P 500 names this year, falling to 6.5% in 2026. Genter expects tariffs to have just a temporary impact on inflation, but to be constantly negotiated and keep some uncertainty in the air. The new environment has also made his firm's fixed-income strategy change from a concentration in short and long maturities to one more focused in the belly of the curve, "in the six-, seven-year area," he says. Genter also worries President Trump's tax-and-spending bill could hurt healthcare municipal bonds. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

Investors are likely to keep an eye on the following small-cap stocks listed on the Australian Securities Exchange.

Talisman Mining

Talisman Mining reported cash on hand of AU$4.5 million and exploration and evaluation expenditure of nearly AU$2 million in the June quarter.

EVZ

EVZ expects its fiscal 2025 revenue to be in the range of AU$108 million to AU$110 million and its earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization to range from AU$5.2 million to AU$5.4 million.

Golden State Mining

Golden State Mining said it is reviewing several assets based on technical merit, short-term drilling feasibility, and low sovereign risk, with a focus on projects in Western Australia, in addition to ongoing exploration planning and activities.

S2 Resources

S2 Resources found a zone of gold-arsenic-antimony anomalism of up to 0.8 grams per tonne grade of gold with the receipt of assays from the three wide-spaced reconnaissance air-core drilling traverses at the Fosterville project in Victoria.

Mount Hope Mining

Mount Hope Mining received all regulatory approvals for the maiden drilling program at its Mt Hope project in New South Wales, allowing for exploration across three priority targets.

data.title: Select Harvests Target Price Cut 17% to A$4.02/Share by Wilsons>SHV.AU
Business Confidence in Australia Rises in Q2 Despite Easing Conditions
data.description:
Business confidence in Australia lifted in the second quarter despite softer conditions, weaker employment, and ongoing pressure on profitability, according to a survey by the National Australia Bank (NAB) published Thursday.

The NAB business confidence index ticked up 3 points to negative 1 index point, its highest level since late 2022, driven by gains in mining, finance, construction, and transport, despite continued weakness in retail and wholesale.

Meanwhile, the NAB business conditions index eased 1 point to nil as trading conditions and employment softened slightly, and profitability remained in negative territory.

Business conditions fell sharply in finance and retail, while mining saw a strong rebound alongside transport, business services, and property gains, per the report. Retail, manufacturing, and wholesale remained the weakest sectors.

Business conditions eased in New South Wales, fell slightly in Queensland and South Australia, and improved in Victoria and Western Australia, though both stayed negative. Conditions remain weakest in South Australia and Victoria, and strongest in Queensland.

Leading indicators showed modest gains with expected business conditions, forward orders, and capex plans rising, capacity utilisation falling, and output constraints easing. In contrast, labor constraints remain a minor issue by historical standards, the report added.

Cost growth remained steady with a small rise in labour costs and a slight drop in purchase costs; retail price growth slowed to its lowest since early 2021, while wage costs and margin pressures continued to weigh on business confidence.

data.title: Austrian Privacy Group Lodges Complaint Against China's AliExpress, TikTok, WeChat Over Data Access
data.description:
Austrian privacy advocacy group noyb has lodged data privacy complaints against Alibaba Group's AliExpress, TikTok and Tencent Holdings' WeChat, according to a press release on Thursday.

The complaints allege that the companies have failed to comply with European Union laws regarding user access to their personal data.

The lobby group said TikTok only provided a portion of the complainant's data "in an unstructured form that was impossible to understand," while AliExpress provided a "broken file" that could only be opened once.

It added that Tencent's messaging and social media app WeChat completely ignored the complainant's request.

data.title: Canada sees growing appetite for conversations on Mercosur trade pact, minister says
data.description:
Canada's International Trade Minister said on Thursday that there was an appetite from both sides to advance conversations on a trade deal with South American bloc Mercosur, as Canada seeks trade pacts globally in push to diversify from the U.S.

Canada is also keen to continue talks with China to address trade challenges and sees a thawing of relations between India and Canada as an important step to support trade, Minister Maninder Sidhu said in an interview with Reuters.

data.title: South Korea's Kospi Opens 0.3% Higher at 3200.44
Nikkei Stock Average Opens 0.4% Higher at 40072.39, Led by Financial Stocks
Fed's Waller wants July interest rate cut amid rising growth, job market risks
data.description:
Key points:
  • Fed's Waller says July rate cut justified by rising risks
  • Waller says trade tariffs unlikely to push up inflation long run
  • Waller said risks mounting for job market, which calls for Fed response

Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said on Thursday he continues to believe the U.S. central bank should cut interest rates at the end of this month amid mounting risks to the economy and the strong likelihood that tariff-induced inflation will not drive a persistent rise in price pressures.

“It makes sense to cut the FOMC’s policy rate by 25 basis points two weeks from now,” Waller told a gathering of the Money Marketeers of New York University.

“I see the hard and soft data on economic activity and the labor market as consistent: The economy is still growing, but its momentum has slowed significantly, and the risks to the (Federal Open Market Committee’s) employment mandate have increased,” and that justifies cutting rates, he said.

All the evidence suggests the Fed can look through the impact of tariffs and focus on other issues affecting the economy, he added.

The Fed's next policy meeting is scheduled for July 29-30.

Waller is one of two Fed officials who have expressed interest in cutting rates this month, reckoning the import tax surge will be a one-time event that policy makers can look through.

A July easing could be followed by more rate cuts, as the Fed no longer needs a monetary policy stance designed to slow the economy, Waller said, noting the Fed’s interest rate target is well above the 3% officials consider its long-run level.

If underlying inflation remains in check and expectations of future price increases stay contained amid slow growth, “I would support further 25 basis point cuts to move monetary policy toward neutral,” he said.

A neutral level rate is considered neither contractionary nor expansionary.

The last time the Fed cut rates was in December 2024, when it trimmed its policy rate by 25 basis points.

Waller warned that not easing this month could create issues down the road.

“If we cut our target range in July and subsequent employment and inflation data point toward fewer cuts, we would have the option of holding policy steady for one or more meetings,” Waller said. But if economic weakness accelerated, “waiting until September or even later in the year would risk us falling behind the curve of appropriate policy,” he said.

Fed policy is not on a preset course and decisions on where to set rates would be done meeting-by-meeting, he added.

Waller is one of the last central bank officials to weigh in on the economy as policy makers go into their customary quiet period ahead of the policy meeting at the end of this month.

Most central bank officials who have spoken have signaled no interest in changing the Fed’s 4.25% to 4.5% interest rate target now as inflation remains above target, the economy is generally faring well, and it’s unclear how much upward price pressure President Donald Trump’s trade tariffs will create.

Financial markets are currently pricing in a September starting date for rate cuts and Fed officials penciled in two easings at their June meeting.

Waller stressed in recent remarks his interest in cutting rates soon was “not political.” Waller is widely viewed as in the running to succeed Fed Chairman Jerome Powell, who has been under regular attack from Trump for not cutting rates.

Waller was asked at the gathering whether anyone from the Trump administration had approached him about becoming Fed chair. He replied "no," without elaboration.

On Wednesday reports indicated the president was close to firing Powell amid a lack of clarity over whether the action would be legal, with Trump later denying those reports.

The Fed has also been in the crosshairs of the administration over cost overruns involving its headquarters, and Waller said that it was not uncommon for these types of projects to see something like this happen.

In his remarks, Waller noted data points to a job market that is “on the edge” of trouble. At the same time, he said that if 10% tariffs were sustained, that would add only 0.75% to 1% to inflation.

Waller also said the cost of paying for Trump's import tax surge will be split: Consumers will pay for a third of the increase, with the remainder split between foreign goods producers and the importers of those products.

data.title: Oil Futures Consolidate Amid Mixed Signals — Market Talk
data.description: Oil futures consolidate in the morning Asian session amid mixed signals. On the demand side, economic resilience in the U.S. and China has provided some support, Tickmill's Joseph Dahrieh says in an email. However, this has been tempered by large increases in gasoline and diesel stockpiles, casting doubt on the strength of fuel demand as the peak summer travel season progresses, the managing principal adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are little changed at $67.51/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are steady at $69.50/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Lost Money on Centene Corporation (CNC)? Join Class Action Before September 8, 2025 - Contact Levi & Korsinsky

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Centene Corporation that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between December 12, 2024 and June 30, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: According to the complaint, defendants provided overwhelmingly positive statements to investors while, at the same time, disseminating materially false and misleading statements and/or concealing material adverse facts concerning the true state of Centene's enrollment and morbidity rates. Investors began to question the veracity of defendants' public statements on July 1, 2025, when Centene issued a press release withdrawing 2025 guidance. Particularly, following an analysis of the 2025 Health Insurance Marketplace, Centene's overall market growth across 22 states, or 72% of the Company's marketplace membership, was lower than expected. In pertinent part, the Company stated that this preliminary analysis resulted in a reduction of its previously issued guidance to approximately $1.8 billion or an adjusted diluted EPS of $2.75.

Following this news, Centene's common stock declined dramatically, from a closing market price of $56.65 per share on July 1, 2025, Centene's stock price fell to $33.78 per share on July 2, 2025, a decline of 40.4%.

data.title: BHP Group's Copper Production Rises 2% in Fiscal Q4
data.description:
BHP Group reported copper production of 516,200 tonnes in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, up 2% year on year and 1% sequentially, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

Iron ore output was 70.3 million tonnes, up 2% from a year earlier and 14% from the fiscal third quarter, the filing said.

In fiscal 2025, the company produced 2 million tonnes of copper and 263 million tonnes of iron ore, up 8% and 1%, respectively, from a year earlier.

For fiscal 2026, BHP said it expects copper production in the range of 1.8 million to 2 million tonnes and iron ore output of 258 million to 269 million tonnes, the filing added.
